intro.title: Part VII as a battlefield manual
intro.subtitle: Use this section to answer sight, cover, engagement, and marker/token questions during play. Open the full Part VII text below when you need the exact source wording.

foundations.title: Line of Sight Core
foundations.summary: Trace a straight line from any part of the acting base to any part of the target base using the game’s top-down 2D system.
bullets: Start with visibility first, then check whether terrain actually blocks the shot.
Base posture and miniature silhouette do not matter; the base is what the rules see.

foundations.title: Cover and Blocking Terrain
foundations.summary: Terrain only matters when it meets the cover requirements. A line crossing terrain does not automatically mean the target is hidden.
bullets: Use cover to decide whether terrain gives Full Cover, Direct Cover, or no meaningful block at all.
Effective Size and elevation decide whether a terrain piece actually matters for the shot.

foundations.title: Engagement State
foundations.summary: Engagement governs who is tied up in combat, who can shoot freely, and who controls combat space.
bullets: Ask first whether the unit is engaged before checking many shooting and movement restrictions.
Engagement is one of the fastest ways to make a target illegal even when it is visible.

systems.title: Line of Sight
systems.purpose: Decide whether a model can see a target and whether a shot is even legal to start.
notes: Terrain in the line only matters when the cover rules say it matters.

procedures.title: Visibility Check
procedures.what: The core test for whether an acting model can see a target model.
procedures.when: Whenever a shot, ability, or rule requires line of sight.
how: Trace a straight line from any part of the acting model’s base to any part of the target model’s base.
Check whether the line passes through blocking terrain.
If it does, use the cover rules to decide whether the terrain actually blocks the shot or whether the target stays visible.
cannot: Assume any crossed terrain automatically blocks the shot.
Use miniature pose, weapon barrel, or head position instead of the base.

procedures.title: Terrain Footprint
procedures.what: The area a terrain piece occupies for sight and cover checks.
procedures.when: Any time a line crosses terrain or a model interacts with openings and interiors.
how: Use the terrain’s physical base, or if it has no base, the outermost solid edges viewed from above.
Treat windows, doorways, gaps, and interiors according to what players agreed during battlefield setup.
Keep the same footprint ruling consistent for the whole game.
cannot: Assume movement openings and line-of-sight openings are always the same thing.

systems.title: Cover and Effective Size
systems.purpose: Decide whether terrain blocks the shot completely, partially, or not at all.
notes: Sight and cover are linked; terrain crossing alone is not enough.
High ground and effective size are where most arguments happen.

procedures.title: Cover Decision
procedures.what: The test that decides whether terrain gives Full Cover, Direct Cover, or no block.
procedures.when: After the line-of-sight trace crosses terrain.
how: Check whether the terrain and target relationship meets the Full Cover rule.
If not, check whether it meets the Direct Cover rule.
If neither applies, the target remains visible despite the terrain crossing.
cannot: Skip the cover step and declare the target hidden immediately.
Ignore effective size when checking whether terrain really matters.

procedures.title: High Ground Reading
procedures.what: The part of the cover system that determines how elevation changes visibility and protection.
procedures.when: When the acting model and target are on different elevations or terrain pieces interact with height.
how: Check the model and terrain effective sizes first.
Then apply the specific high-ground cover rule if the target would otherwise benefit from terrain.
Keep the 2D tracing method, then use height rules only where the cover text calls for them.

systems.title: Engagement and Engagement Range
systems.purpose: Determine when units are tied up in combat and when engagement-based restrictions apply.
notes: Always ask whether a unit is engaged before checking many assault and shooting questions.
One engaged model can make the whole unit count as engaged under the rules framework.

procedures.title: Engagement Check
procedures.what: The test for whether enemy units are in engagement range and therefore locked into combat logic.
procedures.when: During movement, assault, combat, and any rules question that depends on being engaged or unengaged.
how: Measure engagement range using the game’s standard horizontal measurement method.
Apply the engagement restrictions for ground units, terrain, and elevation.
Then decide whether the unit counts as engaged for other rules.
cannot: Assume ranged combat alone makes a unit engaged.
Ignore terrain and elevation restrictions when checking whether bases really engage.

systems.title: Markers and Tokens
systems.purpose: Handle mission markers, force fields, faction indicators, and other battlefield objects consistently.
notes: Tokens and markers are physical game objects with their own footprint logic.
Measure to their nearest physical edge unless a rule says otherwise.

procedures.title: Token Handling
procedures.what: How battlefield tokens function as physical objects for measurement and interaction.
procedures.when: When a rule creates or references a token such as a force field or indicator.
how: Use the token’s physical edge for range and placement checks.
Treat the token according to its own rule text plus the battlefield token rules.
Do not invent line-of-sight or cover behavior unless the token’s rules give it.
